bins.name: FERC Jurisdiction Affirmed
bins.desc: LS Power agrees that large-load interconnection to the interstate transmission grid falls within FERC's exclusive jurisdiction under the Federal Power Act and Supreme Court precedent. The company supports using this jurisdictional authority to reform existing interconnection frameworks rather than building parallel structures.
quotes: the interconnection of large load customers to the interstate transmission grid is squarely within the Commission's jurisdiction.

The Commission should integrate new large loads into the Commission-jurisdictional transmission grid through revisions to existing regional transmission planning and transmission interconnection frameworks rather than establishing additional ad hoc, piecemeal processes solely for large-load interconnections.

bins.name: Large Load Definition Needs Work
bins.desc: LS Power supports the 20 MW threshold direction but calls for a substantive comment period to develop a clearer standard, whether based on grid impact or load megawatts, and to address regional variations and the meaning of 'new' load.
quotes: New large load must be well-defined. The Commission must define it clearly, based on an agreed upon standard, such as whether new large load would have a significant impact to the transmission and energy supply system or based upon load megawatts.

bins.name: Joint Study of Load and Generation
bins.desc: LS Power supports requiring joint interconnection studies for hybrid facilities that interconnect electrically close together. Coordinated study ensures upgrades are sized correctly, avoids redundant infrastructure, and supports resource adequacy analysis.
quotes: The Commission should require that hybrid facility (large load and associated matching generation) interconnections with the interstate transmission grid be studied together where they are anticipated to interconnect electrically close to each other.

bins.name: Standardized Pro-Forma Terms
bins.desc: LS Power supports standardized deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties built on the existing generation interconnection pro-forma, with added provisions for cost and schedule accountability drawn from competitive Designated Entity Agreements.
quotes: The existing generation interconnection frameworks should be used and improved upon to establish standardized pro-forma terms for large load interconnections.

bins.name: Hybrid Rights Tied to Matched Generation
bins.desc: LS Power urges that expedited interconnection rights for hybrid facilities be conditioned on the load bringing matched generation sufficient to cover its full capacity and energy needs, including through accreditation methodologies where applicable. Curtailable hybrids on deferred-upgrade paths must stay within their generation-load balance.
quotes: It is critically important for the Commission to incent large load to bring its own generation (referred to in the Proposed ANOPR as "hybrid facilities"), and we urge the Commission to craft final rules with this in mind.

Large load interconnection rules should provide an expedited process for generation paired with co-located new large load. In order to qualify for expedited interconnection consideration, the large load must bring generation that matches its maximum capacity and energy needs.

Hybrid facilities interconnecting on an expedited basis (deferred transmission upgrades) can be fully curtailable. If associated generation is not providing sufficient energy for its load, then the load cannot look to the grid for its energy and if load is less than the generation supply, the generation may not come onto the grid (other than emergency dispatch).

bins.name: Market Integrity Protections
bins.desc: LS Power calls for rules that prevent curtailment of flexible large loads from suppressing competitive energy market price signals and from distorting resource adequacy planning. Open-access principles prohibit discriminatory treatment of new large load relative to existing load or generation.
quotes: it is also critical that the relevant regional energy markets send the appropriate price signal to indicate scarcity of supply resources such that the curtailment of flexible large loads cannot be used to undermine competitive energy market pricing by failing to reflect curtailable load in resource adequacy planning.
To introduce resource adequacy as a consideration in determining whether and at what cost to interconnect new large load would necessarily discriminate against that new load and existing generators.

bins.name: Expedited Process for Matched Hybrids
bins.desc: LS Power supports an expedited interconnection path but says it should be limited to facilities where generation fully matches the load's capacity and energy needs. The expedited track for curtailable facilities must include operational constraints and must not allow curtailment to undermine market price signals.
quotes: Large load interconnection rules should provide an expedited process for generation paired with co-located new large load. In order to qualify for expedited interconnection consideration, the large load must bring generation that matches its maximum capacity and energy needs.

Hybrid facilities interconnecting on an expedited basis (deferred transmission upgrades) can be fully curtailable. If associated generation is not providing sufficient energy for its load, then the load cannot look to the grid for its energy and if load is less than the generation supply, the generation may not come onto the grid (other than emergency dispatch).

bins.name: Cost Causation for Network Upgrades
bins.desc: LS Power supports the beneficiary-pays principle: the large-load customer bears full cost responsibility for any network upgrades triggered by its interconnection. The company also calls for just and reasonable compensation for transmission owners required to own and maintain those facilities, citing Ameren Services Co. v. FERC.
quotes: when a new large load requires system or network upgrades, the new large load customer should bear ultimate cost responsibility consistent with cost causation and beneficiary pays principles.

Attracting the long-term capital needed to meet the infrastructure needs created by the surge in large-load demand requires that all infrastructure investments receive just and reasonable compensation, established to the maximum possible extent through independent planning and competition.

bins.name: Use Existing Regional Frameworks
bins.desc: LS Power consistently argues that large-load interconnection should be integrated into existing Order No. 1000/1920 regional planning and generation interconnection study processes, not handled through separate ad hoc mechanisms. This ensures consistent modeling, coordinated protection, and NERC compliance.
quotes: The Commission should integrate new large loads into the Commission-jurisdictional transmission grid through revisions to existing regional transmission planning and transmission interconnection frameworks rather than establishing additional ad hoc, piecemeal processes solely for large-load interconnections.

The Commission should require that hybrid facility (large load and associated matching generation) interconnections with the interstate transmission grid be studied together where they are anticipated to interconnect electrically close to each other.
The existing generation interconnection frameworks should be used and improved upon to establish standardized pro-forma terms for large load interconnections.

Large-load interconnection should be integrated into existing planning and study processes for generation interconnection. Large-load integration should be handled within the same coordinated regional planning and study framework used for generation and reliability.

bins.name: Competition to Reduce Interconnection Costs
bins.desc: LS Power urges FERC to use competitive processes for network upgrades above 100 kV identified through the interconnection process, and to allow interconnection customers to assign or compete build rights to qualified transmission developers. All infrastructure must receive just and reasonable compensation to attract long-term capital.
quotes: when a new large load requires system or network upgrades, the new large load customer should bear ultimate cost responsibility consistent with cost causation and beneficiary pays principles.

Attracting the long-term capital needed to meet the infrastructure needs created by the surge in large-load demand requires that all infrastructure investments receive just and reasonable compensation, established to the maximum possible extent through independent planning and competition.

bins.name: Co-location Rules and SSR/RMR Opposition
bins.desc: LS Power supports incentivizing co-located generation and studying load and supply together. However, it firmly opposes ANOPR Principle 10's proposal to apply SSR/RMR-type analysis to existing generation serving co-located data center load, arguing this wrongly treats co-location as a partial suspension and discriminates against new large load.
quotes: It is critically important for the Commission to incent large load to bring its own generation (referred to in the Proposed ANOPR as "hybrid facilities"), and we urge the Commission to craft final rules with this in mind.

Large load interconnection rules should provide an expedited process for generation paired with co-located new large load. In order to qualify for expedited interconnection consideration, the large load must bring generation that matches its maximum capacity and energy needs.

The Commission should require that hybrid facility (large load and associated matching generation) interconnections with the interstate transmission grid be studied together where they are anticipated to interconnect electrically close to each other.
LS Power does not support this principle and supports the EPSA position on this principle.

EPSA rejects the underlying premise that existing generation that is moved behind the meter to serve data center load is akin to a partial suspension or otherwise warrants RMR/SSR type analysis for deactivating resources.
To introduce resource adequacy as a consideration in determining whether and at what cost to interconnect new large load would necessarily discriminate against that new load and existing generators.

bins.name: Flexibility Rules Must Preserve Market Signals
bins.desc: LS Power supports integrating demand response frameworks into large-load interconnection agreements for flexible loads. Curtailment rights for hybrid facilities must be bounded by generation-load balance constraints, and resource adequacy planning must reflect curtailable load so that flexibility does not distort competitive price signals.
quotes: Hybrid facilities interconnecting on an expedited basis (deferred transmission upgrades) can be fully curtailable. If associated generation is not providing sufficient energy for its load, then the load cannot look to the grid for its energy and if load is less than the generation supply, the generation may not come onto the grid (other than emergency dispatch).

it is also critical that the relevant regional energy markets send the appropriate price signal to indicate scarcity of supply resources such that the curtailment of flexible large loads cannot be used to undermine competitive energy market pricing by failing to reflect curtailable load in resource adequacy planning.

bins.name: Co-location Is Not Deactivation
bins.desc: LS Power, citing EPSA, opposes ANOPR Principle 10's premise that moving existing generation behind the meter to serve data center load constitutes a partial suspension. Any reliability review should be limited to safe and reliable interconnection, not broad resource adequacy analysis.
quotes: LS Power does not support this principle and supports the EPSA position on this principle.

EPSA rejects the underlying premise that existing generation that is moved behind the meter to serve data center load is akin to a partial suspension or otherwise warrants RMR/SSR type analysis for deactivating resources.

bins.name: PJM as Reference Example
bins.desc: LS Power references PJM's Necessary Study process as an example of an appropriate limited reliability assessment for co-located or hybrid arrangements, distinct from the broader SSR/RMR-type analysis the ANOPR proposes.
quotes: Large-load interconnection should be integrated into existing planning and study processes for generation interconnection. Large-load integration should be handled within the same coordinated regional planning and study framework used for generation and reliability.
